verses.arabic_full: ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْحَىُّ ٱلْقَيُّومُ ۚ لَا تَأْخُذُهُۥ سِنَةٌ وَلَا نَوْمٌ ۚ لَّهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ مَن ذَا ٱلَّذِى يَشْفَعُ عِندَهُۥٓ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِۦ ۚ يَعْلَمُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ ۖ وَلَا يُحِيطُونَ بِشَىْءٍ مِّنْ عِلْمِهِۦٓ إِلَّا بِمَا شَآءَ ۚ وَسِعَ كُرْسِيُّهُ ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ ۖ وَلَا يَـُٔودُهُۥ حِفْظُهُمَا ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَلِىُّ ٱلْعَظِيمُ
verses.translation: Allah - there is no deity except Him, the Ever-Living, the Sustainer of [all] existence. Neither drowsiness overtakes Him nor sleep. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. Who is it that can intercede with Him except by His permission? He knows what is [presently] before them and what will be after them, and they encompass not a thing of His knowledge except for what He wills. His Kursi extends over the heavens and the earth, and their preservation tires Him not. And He is the Most High, the Most Great.

story.reason: Ayat al-Kursi — majestic description of Allah's sovereignty. Most memorized ayah after Al-Fatiha.

familiarity.reason: Ayat al-Kursi is the single most famous and recited individual ayah. Recited daily after every salah by millions.

teaching_fit.reason: Root in 'la nawm' (no sleep overtakes Him). Powerful usage but root is one detail in a 58-word verse. Would need heavy trimming.

verses.arabic_full: أَفَأَمِنَ أَهْلُ ٱلْقُرَىٰٓ أَن يَأْتِيَهُم بَأْسُنَا بَيَٰتًا وَهُمْ نَآئِمُونَ
verses.translation: Then, did the people of the cities feel secure from Our punishment coming to them at night while they were asleep?

story.reason: Rhetorical warning: did the people feel safe from punishment at night while asleep? Some dramatic tension.

familiarity.reason: Tier D — Al-A'raf, not commonly recited.

teaching_fit.reason: Form na'imun (sleeping) appears clearly. Verse is a warning, sleep provides the dramatic setting.

verses.arabic_full: إِذْ يُرِيكَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ فِى مَنَامِكَ قَلِيلًا ۖ وَلَوْ أَرَىٰكَهُمْ كَثِيرًا لَّفَشِلْتُمْ وَلَتَنَٰزَعْتُمْ فِى ٱلْأَمْرِ وَلَٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَلَّمَ ۗ إِنَّهُۥ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ
verses.translation: [Remember, O Muhammad], when Allah showed them to you in your dream as few; and if He had shown them to you as many, you [believers] would have lost courage and would have disputed in the matter [of whether to fight], but Allah saved [you from that]. Indeed, He is Knowing of that within the breasts.

story.reason: Battle of Badr — Allah showed the enemy as few in the Prophet's dream to give the believers courage. Iconic event.

familiarity.reason: Tier D — Al-Anfal, not commonly recited outside study.

teaching_fit.reason: Form manamika (your dream/sleep) appears but verse is about the Badr vision, not sleep itself. 22 words.

verses.arabic_full: وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلَّيْلَ لِبَاسًا وَٱلنَّوْمَ سُبَاتًا وَجَعَلَ ٱلنَّهَارَ نُشُورًا
verses.translation: And it is He who has made the night for you as clothing and sleep [a means for] rest and has made the day a resurrection.

story.reason: Listing divine blessings: night as clothing, sleep as rest, day as resurrection. Contemplative, no narrative.

familiarity.reason: Tier D — Al-Furqan, not commonly recited.

teaching_fit.reason: Root (al-nawma) is central — verse is explicitly about sleep as a divine blessing. 11 words, self-contained, clear.

verses.arabic_full: وَمِنْ ءَايَٰتِهِۦ مَنَامُكُم بِٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ وَٱبْتِغَآؤُكُم مِّن فَضْلِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَسْمَعُونَ
verses.translation: And of His signs is your sleep by night and day and your seeking of His bounty. Indeed in that are signs for a people who listen.

story.reason: Listing signs of Allah — sleep and seeking bounty. Contemplative but no narrative.

familiarity.reason: Tier D — Ar-Rum, not commonly recited.

teaching_fit.reason: Root (manamukum) is central — the verse is explicitly about sleep as a sign of Allah. Good teaching verse.

verses.arabic_full: فَلَمَّا بَلَغَ مَعَهُ ٱلسَّعْىَ قَالَ يَٰبُنَىَّ إِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰ فِى ٱلْمَنَامِ أَنِّىٓ أَذْبَحُكَ فَٱنظُرْ مَاذَا تَرَىٰ ۚ قَالَ يَٰٓأَبَتِ ٱفْعَلْ مَا تُؤْمَرُ ۖ سَتَجِدُنِىٓ إِن شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ مِنَ ٱلصَّٰبِرِينَ
verses.translation: And when he reached with him [the age of] exertion, he said, "O my son, indeed I have seen in a dream that I [must] sacrifice you, so see what you think." He said, "O my father, do as you are commanded. You will find me, if Allah wills, of the steadfast."

story.reason: Ibrahim tells Ismail he saw in a dream he must sacrifice him. Ismail responds with patient submission. THE most iconic father-son story in Islam.

familiarity.reason: Tier C — As-Saffat. This specific ayah is very well-known from Eid al-Adha context.

teaching_fit.reason: Form al-manam (the dream) is present. But the verse is about sacrifice, not sleep/dreaming per se. 28 words, needs trimming.

verses.arabic_full: ٱللَّهُ يَتَوَفَّى ٱلْأَنفُسَ حِينَ مَوْتِهَا وَٱلَّتِى لَمْ تَمُتْ فِى مَنَامِهَا ۖ فَيُمْسِكُ ٱلَّتِى قَضَىٰ عَلَيْهَا ٱلْمَوْتَ وَيُرْسِلُ ٱلْأُخْرَىٰٓ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ
verses.translation: Allah takes the souls at the time of their death, and those that do not die [He takes] during their sleep. Then He keeps those for which He has decreed death and releases the others for a specified term. Indeed in that are signs for a people who give thought.

story.reason: Profound concept: Allah takes souls at death and during sleep, keeps some and releases others. Deep, memorable teaching.

familiarity.reason: Tier D — Az-Zumar, not commonly recited. But this ayah is cited in Islamic teachings about sleep and death.

teaching_fit.reason: Form manamiha (their sleep) present. Verse connects sleep and death — teaches the root in a deeper context. 28 words, needs trimming.

verses.arabic_full: فَطَافَ عَلَيْهَا طَآئِفٌ مِّن رَّبِّكَ وَهُمْ نَآئِمُونَ
verses.translation: So there came upon the garden an affliction from your Lord while they were asleep.

story.reason: Story of the garden owners — divine punishment came while they slept. Dramatic irony, vivid narrative.

familiarity.reason: Tier C — Al-Qalam is in Juz 29, somewhat known. The garden story is less widely memorized.

teaching_fit.reason: Form na'imun (sleeping ones) is the key detail — they were asleep when punishment struck. Root is dramatically central.

verses.translation: And made your sleep [a means for] rest

story.reason: Statement of divine blessing — 'We made your sleep a rest.' No narrative, but gentle emotional tone.

familiarity.reason: Tier B — An-Naba is in Juz 30 and recited regularly. This ayah is part of the well-known blessings sequence.

teaching_fit.reason: Root (nawmakum) is central and the verse is ABOUT sleep. 3 words, perfect anchor candidate.
